narrative_blocks.body: Hezekiah Hooker's wife Lovice Roe Hooker died on September 20, 1852, and Hezekiah died less than four months later, on January 13, 1853, in Danby, New York. They had raised twelve children together, all born in Dryden, New York.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: School teacher; died unmarried at thirty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Myron Hooker was an unmarried school teacher who died on May 5, 1838 at age thirty-two in Dryden, New York — just over a year after his brother James died at thirty. Two brothers, both young and unmarried, both dead within fourteen months of each other.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty with a five-year-old son
narrative_blocks.body: James Hooker died on May 17, 1837 at age thirty, leaving behind his wife Mary Hutchins and their five-year-old son Nelson Armeanest Hooker. Nelson would grow up to serve in the Civil War, wounded at Trevilian Station, Virginia in 1864, and passed his unusual middle name to his own son Ernest Armeanest Hooker.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
WikiTree genealogical research

career.organization: Buttermilk from F.A. Schuyler's creamery, Barton NY
career.notes: Peddled buttermilk in later life per newspaper account.

military_service.unit: Co. E, 1st Regiment New York Dragoons
military_service.notes: Enlisted Aug 4, 1862 at Independence NY. Wounded Jun 12, 1864 at Trevilian Station VA. Mustered out May 22, 1865 at Mower US Army General Hospital Philadelphia.

narrative_blocks.header: Civil War private; wounded at Trevilian Station, 1864
narrative_blocks.body: Nelson Armeanest Hooker enlisted as a private in Company E of the 1st New York Dragoons on August 4, 1862, was wounded at Trevilian Station, Virginia on June 12, 1864 — the largest all-cavalry engagement of the Civil War — and mustered out at Mower General Hospital in Philadelphia on May 22, 1865. In later life he became a peddler of buttermilk from a local creamery in Barton, New York, and died in February 1892.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Harrison died at twenty-one, June 14, 1863
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Hooker Little's son Harrison died on June 14, 1863 at age twenty-one — in the midst of the Civil War, though no military record has been confirmed. Her son Washington was a starred entry in Edward Hooker's genealogy, indicating documented descendants beyond this line.

narrative_blocks.header: Son named William Henry Harrison, after the president
narrative_blocks.body: Washington Little named his only documented son William Henry Harrison Little after the ninth U.S. president, who had died just one month after his inauguration in 1841 — the Harrison household name held cultural weight in 1866 when the boy was born. Washington's wife June Richards died on March 13, though the year is not recorded in the family genealogy.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.body: Harrison Little died on June 14, 1863 at age twenty-one — in the midst of the Civil War — though no military record has been identified. His father Simeon would outlive him by more than thirty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied for the ministry; died on Christmas Day
narrative_blocks.body: Harmon Hooker was studying for the ministry when he died unmarried on Christmas Day, December 25, 1841, at age twenty-three in Dryden, New York. He was the third of his brothers to die young — following James (d.May 1837, age 30) and Myron (d.May 1838, age 32).

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

education.notes: Per UW archives biography. Also taught school for several years before leaving New York in 1846.

career.notes: Taught school for several years before leaving for Wisconsin per UW archives.

career.organization: Fond du Lac Journal, Fond du Lac Wisconsin
career.notes: Co-founded with J.O. Henning. First newspaper established in Fond du Lac County. Sold within six months.

career.organization: Fond du Lac Whig, Fond du Lac Wisconsin
career.notes: Bought half interest with J.M. Gellett after selling Journal. Sold interest January 1848 and moved to Waupun.

Read law in Waupun from 1848. Admitted to bar Aug 6, 1850 per personal diary (USBD says Apr 17, 1854 — discrepancy). Practiced ~40 years. Traveled on horseback across Dodge County to court in Juneau; sometimes stayed overnight at inn between Minnesota Junction and Juneau.
Active as lobbyist in 1850s per Waupun city history records.
Published Republican paper 1866; sold personal interest 1867 but remained corresponding editor. Firm Hooker, Bell & Hooker continued until paper was sold in 1894.
Operated printing press alongside law practice; with son Culver ran printing business for many years.
With others formed oil company 1866; sold oil by the barrel from West Virginia well.

narrative_blocks.header: Lawyer who published Fond du Lac's first newspaper
narrative_blocks.body: Eli Hooker settled in Fond du Lac, Wisconsin in 1846, co-founded the city's first newspaper the Fond du Lac Journal, and became a Waupun attorney who rode horseback across Dodge County to court in Juneau — sometimes staying overnight at an inn between Minnesota Junction and Juneau. He attended the 1860 Republican convention in Chicago that nominated Lincoln, gave temperance talks, and died December 10, 1889 — his wife Catherine following exactly one month later.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Son named Henry Hooker Bell for the family
narrative_blocks.body: Viola Adelle Hooker's husband Harry Deal Bell became a member of the family printing firm of Hooker, Bell & Hooker in Waupun, Wisconsin, working alongside her brother Culver E. Hooker. Their son Henry Hooker Bell carried the Hooker surname as his middle name.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Culver E. Hooker, Waupun attorney, editor, and mayor.

education.notes: EH: "educated at Wisconsin State University, graduated 1878."

career.notes: Studied law in father Eli Hooker's office; admitted to bar 1880. Co-principal of longest-running family law firm in Wisconsin (Eli → Culver → Edward).
Editor following father Eli Hooker; firm Hooker, Bell & Hooker sold the Waupun Times in 1894 per Paper Trade Journal.
Head of firm; co-members included brother-in-law Harry Deal Bell.
Per Waupun city records: "C.E. Hooker — 1889, City Mayors." Served as mayor in the same year he lost his father, mother, infant daughter, and wife in 40 days.

career.organization: National Bank of Waupun, Waupun Wisconsin
career.notes: Per Waupun history records: "C.E. Hooker — 1919-1928, Pres. Nat'l Bank Waupun."

narrative_blocks.header: Lost father, mother, and wife in forty days
narrative_blocks.body: Culver E. Hooker lost his father Eli on December 10, 1889, his mother Catherine on January 10, 1890, his infant daughter Laura Belle on January 13, 1890, and his first wife Josephine on January 18, 1890 — four deaths within forty days. Despite these losses, he went on to follow his father into law and become editor and proprietor of the Waupun Times.

narrative_blocks.header: A fruit grower; wife's mother née Vanderbilt
narrative_blocks.body: Franklin Hooker was a fruit grower in Ithaca, New York, and married Melissa Delphine Steele, whose mother's maiden name was Vanderbilt — the same Dutch-New York surname as the famous industrial dynasty, though the family connection is not confirmed. Their two daughters, Carrie Augusta and Emma Kate, were both born in Ithaca.
